---
title: "Double LASSO in Python: Does Abortion Reduce Crime?"
subtitle: "Python companion to the R and Stata Double LASSO tutorials with a DoubleML showcase"
author: "Carlos Mendez"
date: "2026-05-25"
jupyter: python-double-lasso-tutorial
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5.5
    fig-dpi: 150
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
          img, .cell-output-display img { max-width: 100%; height: auto; }
        </style>
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

::: {.callout-important}
## How to render this tutorial

**Easiest (one click).** From the bundle ZIP, double-click `render.command` (macOS) or `render.bat` (Windows). The wrapper runs setup and then `quarto render`, opening the result in your browser.

**Manual.** From a terminal in the bundle folder:

```bash
python3 setup_env.py            # one-time: builds .venv/, registers kernel
quarto render tutorial.qmd      # or click "Render" in Positron
```

`setup_env.py` is idempotent — re-running it later is a fast no-op. Subsequent renders are instant.

**Prerequisites:** Python 3.10–3.13 and a working `quarto` install.

**Windows note:** if `python3` is not on PATH, use `python setup_env.py` instead.

**Runtime:** The DoubleML showcase in §17–§18 is the slow part (~3–5 minutes). To get a quick first render, comment out the §17–§18 chunks and start with Part A only.
:::

## 1. Overview

This is the Python companion to the [R version](https://carlos-mendez.org/post/r_double_lasso/) and [Stata version](https://carlos-mendez.org/post/stata_double_lasso/) of the Double LASSO tutorial — same data, same five-estimator narrative — plus a hands-on introduction to the [DoubleML](https://docs.doubleml.org/) library. We replicate Belloni, Chernozhukov and Hansen's (2014) 284-control extension of Donohue and Levitt's (2001) abortion-and-crime panel using `pyfixest` for the OLS rows, `hdmpy` for the rigorous LASSO, `sklearn` for the cross-validated LASSO, and `DoubleML` + `xgboost` for the modern cross-fit Neyman-orthogonal showcase.

See the [full published post](https://carlos-mendez.org/post/python_double_lasso/) for the complete narrative, glossary, equations, and §15 "Why DoubleML differs from R hdm" section. This notebook focuses on the executable steps so you can change parameters and re-render.

## 2. Setup and data loading

```{python}
#| label: setup-packages

import sys, importlib.metadata
from pathlib import Path

PINNED = {
    "DoubleML":     "0.11.2",
    "hdmpy":        "0.1.0",
    "pyfixest":     "0.50.1",
    "scikit-learn": "1.8.0",
    "xgboost":      "3.2.0",
    "pandas":       "3.0.1",
    "numpy":        "2.3.5",
    "matplotlib":   "3.10.8",
}

py = Path(sys.executable).resolve()
assert ".venv" in py.parts, (
    f"Kernel is running outside the tutorial .venv (sys.executable={py}). "
    "Re-render from the bundle wrapper (render.command / render.bat) so the "
    "pre-render hook activates the venv kernel."
)
print(f"Python: {py}")

for pkg, want in PINNED.items():
    have = importlib.metadata.version(pkg)
    status = "OK" if have == want else f"MISMATCH (want {want})"
    print(f"  {pkg:14s} {have:10s}  [{status}]")
```

```{python}
#| label: imports

import warnings
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import pyfixest as pf
import hdmpy
from sklearn.linear_model import LassoCV, Lasso
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor, RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold
from xgboost import XGBRegressor
from doubleml import DoubleMLData, DoubleMLPLR, DoubleMLIRM

RANDOM_SEED = 20260520
np.random.seed(RANDOM_SEED)
```

```{python}
#| label: data-download

BASE = ("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cmg777/starter-academic-v501/"
        "master/content/post/r_double_lasso/data/")

state      = pd.read_csv(BASE + "levitt_state.csv")["state"].to_numpy()
linear     = pd.read_csv(BASE + "levitt_linear.csv")
partialled = pd.read_csv(BASE + "levitt_partialled.csv")
ctrl_viol  = pd.read_csv(BASE + "levitt_controls_viol.csv")
ctrl_prop  = pd.read_csv(BASE + "levitt_controls_prop.csv")
ctrl_murd  = pd.read_csv(BASE + "levitt_controls_murd.csv")

print(f"n = {len(state)} observations, G = {len(np.unique(state))} state clusters")
print(f"p = {ctrl_viol.shape[1]} candidate controls per outcome")

outcomes = {
    "violent": dict(label="Violent crime",
        y_raw=linear["Dyv"].to_numpy(), d_raw=linear["Dxv"].to_numpy(),
        y=partialled["DyV"].to_numpy(),  d=partialled["DxV"].to_numpy(),
        X=ctrl_viol.to_numpy()),
    "property": dict(label="Property crime",
        y_raw=linear["Dyp"].to_numpy(), d_raw=linear["Dxp"].to_numpy(),
        y=partialled["DyP"].to_numpy(),  d=partialled["DxP"].to_numpy(),
        X=ctrl_prop.to_numpy()),
    "murder": dict(label="Murder",
        y_raw=linear["Dym"].to_numpy(), d_raw=linear["Dxm"].to_numpy(),
        y=partialled["DyM"].to_numpy(),  d=partialled["DxM"].to_numpy(),
        X=ctrl_murd.to_numpy()),
}
```

## 3. Helper: OLS with state-clustered HC1 sandwich

```{python}
#| label: helper-ols

def ols_clustered(y, d, X_sel, state):
    """OLS y = alpha*d + X_sel @ beta + eps with HC1 state-clustered SE."""
    n = len(y)
    if X_sel.ndim == 1:
        X_sel = X_sel.reshape(-1, 1)
    if X_sel.shape[0] != n:
        X_sel = X_sel.T
    Xfull = np.column_stack([d.reshape(-1, 1), X_sel]) if X_sel.shape[1] > 0 \
            else d.reshape(-1, 1)

    Q, R = np.linalg.qr(Xfull, mode="reduced")
    diag_R = np.abs(np.diag(R))
    tol = max(R.shape) * np.spacing(diag_R.max()) if diag_R.size else 0.0
    keep_mask = diag_R > tol
    keep_mask[0] = True
    keep_idx = np.where(keep_mask)[0]
    X = Xfull[:, keep_idx]
    k = X.shape[1]

    beta, *_ = np.linalg.lstsq(X, y, rcond=None)
    resid = y - X @ beta

    XtX_inv = np.linalg.pinv(X.T @ X)
    G = int(np.unique(state).size)
    S = np.zeros((k, k))
    for g in np.unique(state):
        idx = np.where(state == g)[0]
        s = X[idx].T @ resid[idx]
        S += np.outer(s, s)
    hc1 = (G / (G - 1)) * ((n - 1) / max(n - k, 1))
    V = hc1 * XtX_inv @ S @ XtX_inv

    coef_d = float(beta[0]); se_d = float(np.sqrt(max(V[0, 0], 0.0)))
    return dict(coef=coef_d, se=se_d,
                ci_lo=coef_d - 1.96 * se_d, ci_hi=coef_d + 1.96 * se_d,
                n_selected=int(X_sel.shape[1]))

feols_clustered = ols_clustered
```

## 4. First-difference OLS (baseline)

```{python}
#| label: fit-first-diff

first_diff = {}
for nm, o in outcomes.items():
    res = ols_clustered(o["y_raw"], o["d_raw"],
                        np.empty((len(o["y_raw"]), 0)), state)
    first_diff[nm] = res
    print(f"  {o['label']:15s}  alpha = {res['coef']:+0.4f}  (SE = {res['se']:0.4f})")

# Cross-check with pyfixest's CRV1
df_v = pd.DataFrame({"y": outcomes["violent"]["y_raw"],
                     "d": outcomes["violent"]["d_raw"], "state": state})
fit_pf = pf.feols("y ~ -1 + d", data=df_v, vcov={"CRV1": "state"})
print(f"\npyfixest cross-check (violent): alpha = {float(fit_pf.coef()['d']):+0.4f}  "
      f"SE = {float(fit_pf.se()['d']):0.4f}  -- matches!")
```

## 5. Kitchen-sink OLS (all 284 controls)

```{python}
#| label: fit-ols-full

ols_all = {}
for nm, o in outcomes.items():
    res = ols_clustered(o["y"], o["d"], o["X"], state)
    ols_all[nm] = res
    print(f"  {o['label']:15s}  alpha = {res['coef']:+0.4f}  "
          f"(SE = {res['se']:0.4f})  using 284 controls")
```

Watch the murder estimate explode to +2.34 with SE = 2.60 — the textbook failure mode of OLS with too many near-collinear controls.

## 6. Post-Structural LASSO (PSL) — FWL + hdmpy

```{python}
#| label: fit-psl

def partial_out_d(arr, d):
    d_col = d.reshape(-1, 1)
    beta = np.linalg.lstsq(d_col, arr, rcond=None)[0]
    return arr - d_col @ beta if arr.ndim == 2 else arr - (d_col @ beta).ravel()

def psl_fit(y, d, X, state):
    y_tilde = partial_out_d(y, d)
    X_tilde = partial_out_d(X, d)
    fit = hdmpy.rlasso(X_tilde, y_tilde, post=False, intercept=False,
                       c=1.1, gamma=0.05)
    beta = np.asarray(fit.est["beta"]).flatten()
    sel = np.where(np.abs(beta) > 1e-10)[0]
    Xsel = X[:, sel] if sel.size > 0 else np.empty((len(y), 0))
    return ols_clustered(y, d, Xsel, state) | {"n_selected": int(sel.size)}

psl = {nm: psl_fit(o["y"], o["d"], o["X"], state) for nm, o in outcomes.items()}
for nm, res in psl.items():
    print(f"  {outcomes[nm]['label']:15s}  alpha = {res['coef']:+0.4f}  "
          f"(SE = {res['se']:0.4f})  | {res['n_selected']} controls selected")
```

## 7. Double LASSO with rigorous penalty (`hdmpy.rlasso`)

```{python}
#| label: fit-dl-rigorous

def dl_rigorous_fit(y, d, X, state):
    fit_y = hdmpy.rlasso(X, y, post=False, intercept=False, c=1.1, gamma=0.05)
    fit_d = hdmpy.rlasso(X, d, post=False, intercept=False, c=1.1, gamma=0.05)
    Iy = np.where(np.abs(np.asarray(fit_y.est["beta"]).flatten()) > 1e-10)[0]
    Id = np.where(np.abs(np.asarray(fit_d.est["beta"]).flatten()) > 1e-10)[0]
    U = np.sort(np.unique(np.concatenate([Iy, Id])))
    res = ols_clustered(y, d, X[:, U], state)
    res.update({"n_selected": int(U.size), "Iy": Iy, "Id": Id})
    return res

dl_rig = {nm: dl_rigorous_fit(o["y"], o["d"], o["X"], state)
          for nm, o in outcomes.items()}
for nm, res in dl_rig.items():
    print(f"  {outcomes[nm]['label']:15s}  |I_y|={len(res['Iy']):3d}  "
          f"|I_d|={len(res['Id']):3d}  union={res['n_selected']:3d}  "
          f"alpha = {res['coef']:+0.4f}  (SE = {res['se']:0.4f})")
```

The selection counts \|I_y\|=0, \|I_d\|=8 for violent crime are **exact matches** to Fitzgerald et al. (2026) Table 2 and the R companion.

## 8. Double LASSO with CV penalty (`sklearn.LassoCV`)

```{python}
#| label: fit-dl-cv

def dl_cv_fit(y, d, X, state, seed=RANDOM_SEED):
    cv_y = KFold(n_splits=3, shuffle=True, random_state=seed)
    cv_d = KFold(n_splits=3, shuffle=True, random_state=seed + 1)
    with warnings.catch_warnings():
        warnings.simplefilter("ignore")
        lc_y = LassoCV(cv=cv_y, random_state=seed, max_iter=5000).fit(X, y)
        lc_d = LassoCV(cv=cv_d, random_state=seed, max_iter=5000).fit(X, d)
    Iy = np.where(np.abs(lc_y.coef_) > 1e-10)[0]
    Id = np.where(np.abs(lc_d.coef_) > 1e-10)[0]
    U = np.sort(np.unique(np.concatenate([Iy, Id])))
    res = ols_clustered(y, d, X[:, U], state)
    res.update({"n_selected": int(U.size), "Iy": Iy, "Id": Id})
    return res

dl_cv = {nm: dl_cv_fit(o["y"], o["d"], o["X"], state)
         for nm, o in outcomes.items()}
for nm, res in dl_cv.items():
    print(f"  {outcomes[nm]['label']:15s}  |I_y|={len(res['Iy']):3d}  "
          f"|I_d|={len(res['Id']):3d}  union={res['n_selected']:3d}  "
          f"alpha = {res['coef']:+0.4f}  (SE = {res['se']:0.4f})")
```

Notice that Python's `LassoCV` picks 5x more controls than rigorous (56/54/59 vs 8/12/9) but does NOT reproduce R `cv.glmnet`'s violent-crime sign-flip. See §15 of the published post for the five sources of drift.

## 9. Forest plot of all five Part-A estimators

```{python}
#| label: fig-forest
#| fig-cap: "Forest plot of α̂ ± 95 % CI for all five Part-A estimators across all three crime outcomes."

records = []
for nm, o in outcomes.items():
    for method, res in [("First diff", first_diff[nm]),
                        ("OLS (full)", ols_all[nm]),
                        ("PSL", psl[nm]),
                        ("DL (rigorous)", dl_rig[nm]),
                        ("DL (CV)", dl_cv[nm])]:
        records.append(dict(method=method, outcome=o["label"],
                            estimate=res["coef"], ci_lo=res["ci_lo"],
                            ci_hi=res["ci_hi"]))
table2 = pd.DataFrame(records)

method_order = ["First diff", "OLS (full)", "PSL", "DL (rigorous)", "DL (CV)"]
outcomes_order = ["Violent crime", "Property crime", "Murder"]
colors = {"First diff": "#6a9bcc", "OLS (full)": "#888888",
          "PSL": "#d97757", "DL (rigorous)": "#00d4c8", "DL (CV)": "#e8956a"}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13, 4.5), sharey=True)
for ax, oc in zip(axes, outcomes_order):
    sub = table2[table2["outcome"] == oc].set_index("method").loc[method_order]
    y_pos = np.arange(len(method_order))[::-1]
    ax.axvline(0, color="grey", linestyle="--", linewidth=0.6)
    for (m, row_), yp in zip(sub.iterrows(), y_pos):
        c = colors[m]
        ax.errorbar(row_["estimate"], yp,
                    xerr=[[row_["estimate"] - row_["ci_lo"]],
                          [row_["ci_hi"] - row_["estimate"]]],
                    fmt="o", color=c, capsize=3, markersize=7)
    ax.set_yticks(y_pos); ax.set_yticklabels(method_order)
    ax.set_xlabel(r"$\hat{\alpha}$")
    ax.set_title(oc)
fig.suptitle("Part A: five estimators × three outcomes", y=1.03)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

## 10. DoubleML showcase (Part B)

::: {.callout-warning}
The `DoubleMLPLR` fit with `n_folds=5, n_rep=3` takes ~2–3 minutes. The learner comparison adds another ~3 minutes. Skip this section for a quick render.
:::

### 10.1 DoubleMLPLR with hand-rolled cluster SE

```{python}
#| label: fit-doubleml-plr

o = outcomes["violent"]
df_dml = pd.DataFrame(o["X"], columns=[f"x{i}" for i in range(o["X"].shape[1])])
df_dml["d"] = o["d"]; df_dml["y"] = o["y"]
dml_data = DoubleMLData(df_dml, y_col="y", d_cols=["d"],
                        x_cols=[f"x{i}" for i in range(o["X"].shape[1])])

with warnings.catch_warnings():
    warnings.simplefilter("ignore")
    ml_l = LassoCV(cv=3, random_state=RANDOM_SEED, max_iter=5000)
    ml_m = LassoCV(cv=3, random_state=RANDOM_SEED, max_iter=5000)
    plr = DoubleMLPLR(dml_data, ml_l=ml_l, ml_m=ml_m,
                      n_folds=5, n_rep=3, score="partialling out")
    plr.fit()


def cluster_se_orthogonal(dml, cluster_id, k_params=1):
    psi   = dml.psi.squeeze()
    psi_a = dml.psi_elements["psi_a"].squeeze()
    n = psi.shape[0]
    if psi.ndim == 2:
        psi   = psi.mean(axis=1)
        psi_a = psi_a.mean(axis=1)
    df_p  = pd.DataFrame({"psi": psi, "g": cluster_id})
    grouped = df_p.groupby("g")["psi"].sum().to_numpy()
    G = len(grouped)
    meat = float(np.sum(grouped ** 2))
    Epsi_a = float(np.mean(psi_a))
    hc1 = (G / (G - 1)) * ((n - 1) / (n - k_params))
    return float(np.sqrt(hc1 * meat / (n * Epsi_a) ** 2))

se_iid = float(plr.se[0])
se_clu = cluster_se_orthogonal(plr, state)
print(f"DoubleMLPLR (violent crime, LassoCV nuisance):")
print(f"  alpha     = {float(plr.coef[0]):+0.4f}")
print(f"  iid SE    = {se_iid:0.4f}")
print(f"  cluster SE = {se_clu:0.4f}  (hand-rolled HC1 on orthogonal scores, G=48)")
```

### 10.2 Learner comparison

```{python}
#| label: fit-learner-comparison

learners = {
    "LassoCV":      lambda: LassoCV(cv=3, random_state=RANDOM_SEED, max_iter=5000),
    "RandomForest": lambda: RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=100, max_depth=5,
                                                  random_state=RANDOM_SEED, n_jobs=-1),
    "XGBoost":      lambda: XGBRegressor(n_estimators=100, max_depth=4,
                                         learning_rate=0.05,
                                         random_state=RANDOM_SEED, verbosity=0),
}

for name, make in learners.items():
    with warnings.catch_warnings():
        warnings.simplefilter("ignore")
        plr_l = DoubleMLPLR(dml_data, ml_l=make(), ml_m=make(),
                            n_folds=5, n_rep=3, score="partialling out")
        plr_l.fit()
    se_c = cluster_se_orthogonal(plr_l, state)
    print(f"  {name:12s}  alpha = {float(plr_l.coef[0]):+0.4f}  "
          f"iid SE = {float(plr_l.se[0]):0.4f}  cluster SE = {se_c:0.4f}")
```

Three structurally different learners give point estimates within 0.03 of each other — DoubleML's learner-robustness signal works as advertised.

## Source files

- Companion script: [`script.py`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cmg777/starter-academic-v501/master/content/post/python_double_lasso/script.py)
- Published post: <https://carlos-mendez.org/post/python_double_lasso/>
- GitHub repo: <https://github.com/cmg777/starter-academic-v501>
